17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116

120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.

139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart at the pinned commit and must be built here, each in its own
26	section:
27	
28	\begin{enumerate}
29	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
30	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
31	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
32	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
33	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
34	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
35	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
36	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
37	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
38	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
39	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
40	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
41	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
42	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
43	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
44	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
45	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
46	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
47	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
48	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
49	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
50	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
51	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
52	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
53	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
54	    \emph{module} structure on
55	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	
58	\emph{Effort estimate.} This is a net-new infrastructure build comparable in
59	character to the graded Hilbert--Serre toolkit (which ran to roughly nine
60	iterations and about \(1300\) lines once the missing graded-quotient API had to
61	be routed around). The present chapter is, if anything, heavier on the geometric
62	side: \cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers} must descend Mathlib's presheaf-level
63	monoidal structure through sheafification and verify the tensor-power comparison
64	isomorphisms (realistically six or more iterations and the bulk of the line
65	count, since the coherence bookkeeping for
66	\(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \cong
67	\mathcal{L}^{\otimes(m+m')}\) has no Mathlib shortcut);
68	\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections} (the lax structure map plus its coherences) about
69	three; and \cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly} about three more, mostly plumbing into
70	the Mathlib \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\)/\(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) classes. A realistic
71	total is on the order of twelve to fifteen iterations and \(1200\)--\(1600\)
72	lines; the layers are genuinely separable, so they can be scheduled
73	independently and need not block one another.
74	
75	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
76	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
77	
78	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
79	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
80	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
81	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
82	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
83	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
84	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
85	monoidal structure applies.
86	
87	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
88	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
89	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
90	  \mathlibok
91	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
92	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
93	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
94	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
95	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
96	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
97	  and an object \(U\),
98	  \[
99	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
100	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
101	  \]
102	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
103	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
104	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
105	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
106	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
107	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
108	\end{lemma}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
111	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
112	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
113	  \mathlibok
114	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
115	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
116	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
117	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
118	  \[
119	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
120	  \]
121	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
122	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
123	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
124	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
125	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
126	\end{lemma}
127	
128	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
129	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
130	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
131	  \mathlibok
132	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
133	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
134	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
135	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
136	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
137	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
138	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
139	  the section graded ring.
140	\end{lemma}
141	
142	\begin{definition}[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
143	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
145	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
146	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
147	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
148	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
149	  % L2282-L2297).
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
151	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
152	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
153	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
154	  %  $$
155	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
156	  %  $$
157	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
158	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
159	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
160	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
161	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
164	  %  =
165	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
166	  %  $$"
167	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
168	  product).}
169	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
170	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
171	  \[
172	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
173	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
174	  \]
175	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
176	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
177	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
178	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
179	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
180	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
181	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
182	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
183	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
184	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
185	\end{definition}
186	
187	\begin{definition}[Tensor power of a line bundle]
188	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
190	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
191	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
192	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
193	  % L4220-L4228).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
195	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
196	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
197	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
198	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
199	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
200	  %  exactly $n$ times."
201	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
202	  powers).}
203	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
204	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
205	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
206	  on \(m\):
207	  \[
208	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
209	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
210	    \qquad
211	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
212	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
213	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
214	  \]
215	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
216	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
217	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
218	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
219	  invertible.
220	\end{definition}
221	
222	\begin{definition}[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
223	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
225	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
226	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
227	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
228	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
229	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
230	  \[
231	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
232	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
233	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
234	  \]
235	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
236	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
237	  \mathcal{F}\).
238	\end{definition}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
241	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
243	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
244	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
245	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
246	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
247	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
248	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
249	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
250	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
251	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
252	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
253	  %  (formulation omitted)."
254	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
255	  power isomorphisms).}
256	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
257	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
258	  \[
259	    \mu_{m,m'} :
260	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
261	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
262	  \]
263	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
264	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
265	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
266	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
267	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
268	\end{lemma}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
272	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
273	  Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For \(m = 0\),
274	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} =
275	  \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}\)
276	  is the left unitor of the tensor product
277	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}, descended through
278	  sheafification). For the step, using the recursive definition
279	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
280	  and the associator of the tensor product,
281	  \[
282	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
283	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
284	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
285	    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
286	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
287	    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
288	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
289	  \]
290	  and the inductive comparison together with one more application of the
291	  recursive definition identifies this with
292	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L} =
293	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)}\). The commutativity and associativity
294	  constraints follow from the corresponding coherence pentagon and hexagon of
295	  the symmetric monoidal structure on \(\PshMod\)
296	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), which are preserved by the
297	  sheafification functor.
298	\end{proof}

300	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
301	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
302	
303	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
304	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
305	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
306	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
307	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
308	global sections.
309	
310	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
311	  \label{def:sectionMul}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
313	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
314	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
315	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
316	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
317	  \[
318	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
319	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
320	    \;\longrightarrow\;
321	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
322	  \]
323	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
324	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
325	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
326	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
327	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
328	  the sheafification unit
329	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
330	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
331	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
332	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
333	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
334	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
335	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
336	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
337	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
338	\end{definition}
339	
340	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
341	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
342	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
343	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
344	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
345	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
346	  the two composites
347	  \[
348	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
349	    \longrightarrow
350	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
351	    \cong
352	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
353	  \]
354	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
355	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
356	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
357	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
358	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
359	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
364	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
365	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
366	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
367	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
368	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
369	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
370	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
371	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
372	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
373	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
374	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
375	  section multiplications.
376	\end{proof}

378	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
379	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
380	
381	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
382	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
383	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
384	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
385	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
386	
387	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
388	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
389	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
390	  \mathlibok
391	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
392	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
393	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
394	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
395	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
396	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
397	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
398	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
399	  \(1 \in A_0\).
400	\end{lemma}
401	
402	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
403	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
404	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
405	  \mathlibok
406	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
407	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
408	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
409	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
410	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
411	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
412	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
413	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
414	  extension of the graded action.
415	\end{lemma}
416	
417	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
418	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
419	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
420	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
421	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
422	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
423	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
424	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
425	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
426	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
427	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
428	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
429	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
430	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
431	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
432	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
433	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
434	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
435	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
436	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
437	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
438	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
439	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
440	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
441	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
442	  multiplication is the composite
443	  \[
444	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
445	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
446	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
447	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
448	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
449	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
450	  \]
451	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
452	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
453	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
454	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
455	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
456	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
457	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
458	\end{lemma}
459	
460	\begin{proof}
461	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
462	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
463	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
464	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
465	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
466	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
467	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
468	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
469	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with

472	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
473	  commutative-semiring structure.

481	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}

16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}

102	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)

112	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module

114	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route

129	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).

132	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
133	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
134	
135	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
136	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
137	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
138	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
139	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
140	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
141	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
142	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
143	
144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
145	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
146	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
148	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
149	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
150	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{proof}
154	
155	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
156	  finitely presented, so
157	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
158	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
159	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
160	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
161	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
162	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
163	  \(f := r\).
164	\end{proof}

472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and

479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing

494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)

506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra

551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the

558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}

581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)

622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
637	\end{lemma}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}
